concepts.title: Magnetic Field Due to Straight Wire
Magnetic Field Due to Circular Loop
concepts.conceptBody:
Magnetic Field Due to Circular Loop 

 

Consider an element $\overrightarrow{\text{dl}}$ of the ring.

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & \overrightarrow{\text{dB}}=\frac{{{\mu }_{0}}}{4\pi }\left( \frac{\text{i}\overrightarrow{\text{dl}}\times \vec{r}}{{{\text{r}}^{3}}} \right) \\ {} & \overrightarrow{\text{dB}}=\frac{{{\mu }_{0}}\text{i}}{4\pi }\left( \frac{\text{dl}\cdot \text{R}}{{{\text{R}}^{3}}} \right) \\ {} & \text{B}=\frac{{{\mu }_{0}}\text{i}}{4\pi {{\text{R}}^{2}}}\int ~\text{dl}=\frac{{{\mu }_{0}}\text{i}}{4\pi {{\text{R}}^{2}}}\left( 2\pi \text{R} \right)=\frac{{{\mu }_{0}}\text{i}}{2\text{R}} \\ \end{array}$

Note: The direction of the $\text{\vec{B}}$ field is perpendicular to the plane of the circular loop and given by the right-hand rule.

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ Magnetic field at the center of a circular ring $=\left( \frac{{{\mu }_{0}}i}{2R} \right)$

Magnetic field at the center of a circular arc subtending angle $\theta $ at the center$=\frac{{{\mu }_{0}}\text{i}}{2\text{R}}\left( \frac{\theta }{2\pi } \right)$

Magnetic Induction due to a circular loop at an axial point. 

We can see from the figure that the vertical component of magnetic fields (due to diametrically opposite lengths dl) will cancel out.

And the net magnetic field will be along x-axis. i.e.

$B=2\int ~\text{dBsin}\theta $ $=\int_{0}^{\pi R} \frac{2 \mu_{0}}{4 \pi}\left(\frac{\mathrm{idl} \times \mathrm{r}}{\mathrm{r}^{3}}\right) \sin \theta$ $=\frac{2{{\mu }_{0}}}{4\pi }\frac{\text{R}}{{{\text{r}}^{3}}}\mathop{\int }_{0}^{\pi \text{R}}\text{dl}$

 

Note : The direction of the $\text{\vec{B}}$ field at the axial position is same throughout and given by the right hand rule.

In the above case when $\mathrm{B}=\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{iR}^2}{2 \mathrm{x}^3}$ 

$=\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{iR}^2}{4 \pi \mathrm{x}^3}(2 \pi)-\left(\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{i}}{4 \pi}\right) 2\left(\frac{\mathrm{i} \pi \mathrm{R}^2}{\mathrm{x}^3}\right)$

$B=\left(\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi}\right)\left(\frac{2 M}{x^3}\right)$ 

[The above result is similar to that of dipoles]

Hence, the above arrangement of circular loop behaves as a magnetic dipole and all the results of the electron dipole are similarly valid.

Note: The above results are valid not only for circular loop but are also valid for any shape of loop when $\text{x}>\mathbf{R}$.

When the dimension of the loop is much smaller in comparison to the distance of the point, then $\text{M}=\text{iA}$

$B=\left( \frac{{{\mu }_{0}}}{4\pi } \right)\left( \frac{2M}{{{x}^{3}}} \right)\text{ } \text{ at } \text{ an } \text{ axial } \text{ point } \text{ }$

for any point $P\left( r,\theta \right)$ is space $B=\frac{{{\mu }_{0}}M}{4\pi }\sqrt{3\text{co}{{\text{s}}^{2}}\theta +1}$

concepts.title: Magnetic Field Due to Infinite Long Current Carrying Wire
concepts.conceptBody:
Magnetic Field Due to Infinite Long Current Carrying Wire

 

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & \oint \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\vec{B}\cdot \overrightarrow{\text{dl}}={{\mu }_{0}}\text{I} \\ {} & ~\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ B}\left( 2\pi \text{r} \right)={{\mu }_{0}}\text{I} \\ {} & ~\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ B}=\frac{{{\mu }_{0}}\text{I}}{2\pi \text{r}} \\  \end{array}$ 

Note that for a symmetrical field the direction of the $\vec{E}$ field is tangent to the path, whereas it is radial in the case of $\vec{E}$ field,

concepts.title: Magnetic Field Due to Long Cylindrical Conductor
concepts.conceptBody:
Field due to along cylindrical conductor of radius $\mathbf{R}$. 

(a) Field inside the conductor, $r\le \mathbf{R}$ : Consider a closed circular loop at a distance $r$ from central axis.

Now, current enclosed by the loop is $\left( \frac{\text{I}}{\pi {{\text{R}}^{2}}} \right)\times \pi {{\text{r}}^{2}}$

 

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ According to Ampere's Law

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & \oint \vec{B}\cdot \overrightarrow{\text{dl}}={{\mu }_{0}}\text{i} \\ \Rightarrow &  \text{ B}\text{. } 2\pi \text{r}={{\mu }_{0}}\frac{\text{I}{{\text{r}}^{2}}}{{{\text{R}}^{2}}} \end{array}$

It can also be written as $ $

 

(b) Outside the cylindrical conductor $(\text{r}>\mathbf{R})$ 

Current enclosed by loop $=\text{I}$

 

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & \oint \vec{B}\cdot \overrightarrow{\text{dl}}={{\mu }_{0}}\text{I} \end{array}$

The field outside the cylinder is also given by,

$\vec{B} = \frac{{\mu_0 R^2}}{2} = \frac{(\vec{j} \times \vec{r})}{|\vec{r}|}$
 

Where $\text{j}$ is the charge density i.e.,

concepts.title: Magnetic Moment of Rotating Charge Body
Magnetic Field Due to Toroid
concepts.conceptBody:
Magnetic Field Due to Toroid

Current in the toroid $=\text{i}$, Total no. of turns = N

$\oint \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{dl}}-\mu_0 \mathrm{I}_{\text {net }}$

$\mathrm{B}(2 \pi r)=\mu_0(\mathrm{iN})$

$\therefore \mathrm{B}=\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{iN}}{2 \pi \mathrm{r}}$

Thus, $\mathrm{B}=0$ for $(\mathrm{r}<\mathrm{a})(\mathrm{r}>\mathrm{b})$

$\mathrm{B}-\left(\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{iN}}{2 \pi \mathrm{I}}\right) \quad$ for $\quad(\mathrm{a} \leq \mathrm{I} \leq \mathrm{b})$

 

$\left( \text{B}\propto \frac{1}{\text{r}} \right)$

However, if $\text{r}$ is very large compared with the cross-sectional radius of the toroid, the field is approximately uniform.

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & \frac{\text{N}}{2\pi \text{r}}=\text{n}=\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ number }\!\!~\!\!\text{ of }\!\!~\!\!\text{ turns }\!\!~\!\!\text{ / }\!\!~\!\!\text{ length }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } \\ {} & \\ \end{array}$

concepts.title: Magnetic Field
concepts.conceptBody:
Concept of Magnetic Field

Consider the situation when a charge placed near an electrical circuit. When the switch is closed and the charge is at rest, then there is no force between the current carrying wire and charged particle. Now, it was experimentally observed that when the charge is set into motion, we observe a force acting on the charge. This suggests that some field is generated by the motion of a charge. This is known as magnetic field.

Also, in case of permanent magnet. We have permanent magnetic field because the electrons present inside the atoms rotate in the same sense.
